%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Learn how to decompose a rational function into simpler partial fractions, and master the most efficient methods for doing so.

Section B.5 covers **partial fraction expansion** — a technique for breaking a complicated rational function (a ratio of polynomials) into a sum of simpler fractions. This matters because simpler fractions are far easier to work with in calculus, signal processing, and control systems.

After completing this section, you will be able to:
- Identify whether a rational function is **proper** (degree of numerator less than denominator)
- Apply the **Method of Clearing Fractions** to find expansion coefficients
- Use the faster **Heaviside Cover-Up Method** for distinct factors
- Handle **repeated factors** in the denominator correctly

The key exam trap: forgetting to check whether the function is proper before expanding.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Partial fraction expansion: one rational function splits into a sum of simple fractions. Two methods — Clearing Fractions and Heaviside Cover-Up — find the coefficients.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777213391908-8385.png)

## Core Idea

The central relationship in B.5 is this: any **proper** rational function (where the degree of the numerator is strictly less than the degree of the denominator) can be written as a **sum of simpler fractions**, one for each factor of the denominator.

For example, if the denominator factors as (x+1)(x−2)(x+3), then:

$$
F(x) = \frac{P(x)}{(x+1)(x-2)(x+3)} = \frac{k_1}{x+1} + \frac{k_2}{x-2} + \frac{k_3}{x+3}
$$

The two main methods for finding the coefficients \(k_1, k_2, k_3\) are:

1. **Clearing Fractions** — multiply both sides by the full denominator, expand, and solve the resulting system of equations. Works in all cases but can be tedious.
2. **Heaviside Cover-Up** — for distinct factors only: to find \(k_i\), mentally cover the factor \((x - r_i)\) in the denominator and substitute \(x = r_i\) into the rest.

### EXAM TRAP

If the rational function is **not proper** (numerator degree ≥ denominator degree), you must perform **polynomial long division first** to extract the polynomial part before expanding the proper remainder.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- A proper rational function can always be decomposed into a sum of simpler partial fractions.
- Clearing Fractions works universally; Heaviside Cover-Up is faster for distinct denominator factors.
- Always check: if the function is not proper, do long division before expanding.

*In the next section, we will see how partial fraction expansion connects directly to inverse Laplace and inverse Z-transforms, where each simple fraction maps to a known time-domain signal.*
